title: Neonatal Sepsis
oneLiner: Bacterial infection in first 28 days of life; rapid deterioration possible; early antibiotics reduce mortality and morbidity.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Congenital heart disease (duct-dependent lesion):**  
  – **Single finding:** Cyanosis that does NOT improve with 100% oxygen (hyperoxia test fails) + differential cyanosis (lower body more blue than upper → coarctation/ interrupted arch).  
  – Immediate action: IV prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) to maintain ductal patency; review echocardiography urgently.
- **Inborn error of metabolism (IEM):**  
  – **Single finding:** Severe metabolic acidosis (base excess < –10) with hypoglycaemia, ketonuria, and normal CRP / WCC (or mild elevation).  
  – Immediate action: stop feeds, give IV dextrose (10% + maintenance rate), check ammonia, lactate, blood gas, and call metabolic team.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Temperature instability (hypothermia or hyperthermia)  
  - Poor feeding / vomiting / feed intolerance  
  - Lethargy / irritability / hypotonia  
  - Respiratory distress (tachypnoea, grunting, desaturations)  
  - Jaundice (especially within first 24 hours or conjugated)  
  - Hypoperfusion (prolonged capillary refill, hypotension)
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Pallor, mottling, cold peripheries  
  - Tachycardia or bradycardia  
  - Bulging fontanelle (late sign of meningitis)  
  - Rash (e.g., pustules → HSV?; petechiae → meningococcus / DIC)  
  - Abdominal distension / hepaticomegaly
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**  
  - **Bacterial sepsis** (GBS, *E. coli*, *Listeria*) → CRP ↑, blood culture +  
  - **Viral infection** (HSV, enterovirus, CMV) → vesicles (HSV), transaminitis (enterovirus), normal CRP initially  
  - **Fungal infection** (Candida in preterm, indwelling lines) → persistent fever, negative bacterial cultures  
  - **Non-infective causes:** IEM, CHD, NEC (abdominal distension + pneumatosis on XR)
- **Splitter:** Presence of clustered vesicles on skin or mucous membranes → think **HSV** (not bacterial); if no vesicles, bacterial is more likely.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Blood culture (ideally before antibiotics, but DO NOT delay antibiotics >30 min)  
  - Blood gas (pH, lactate, base excess, glucose)  
  - CRP (rapid bedside if available)  
  - Urine dipstick (if difficult, consider catheter sample)
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, CRP, blood culture, glucose, electrolytes, renal function, liver function, lactate  
  - Coagulation screen (if petechiae / DIC suspected)  
  - Blood gas for acid–base status  
  - Consider ammonia, acylcarnitine profile if IEM suspected
- **Imaging:**  
  - CXR (look for pneumonia, cardiomegaly, evidence of NEC)  
  - Abdominal XR (if NEC suspected — pneumatosis, portal gas)  
  - Cranial ultrasound (if seizures, bulging fontanelle)
- **Special tests:**  
  - Lumbar puncture (LP) – **strongly consider** if: suspect meningitis, neurological signs, abnormal CSF parameters; **do not delay antibiotics for LP**  
  - Urine culture (clean catch or catheter)  
  - Swabs (skin lesions for HSV PCR, stool culture for enterovirus)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** NICE guideline (NG195) — clinical risk factors + abnormal vital signs. No single score replaces clinical judgment; use the “neonatal early warning score” (NEWS2 modified) but thresholds are gestational-age dependent.
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood culture** from a sterile site (peripheral vein or peripheral line) — if omitted, you cannot confirm aetiology or tailor antibiotics.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - ABCDE approach; secure airway if depressed GCS, 100% O₂ if SpO₂ <88%  
  - IV access (difficult → intraosseous if necessary)  
  - Blood culture (then start antibiotics within 1 hour of recognition)  
  - IV benzylpenicillin + gentamicin (see Drugs & Doses)  
  - Fluid bolus 10–20 mL/kg 0.9% saline [check local policy] if shock (prolonged CRT, tachycardia, hypotension)  
  - Correct hypoglycaemia (IV 10% dextrose 2–3 mL/kg)  
  - Monitor temperature, heart rate, respiratory rate, blood pressure, SpO₂ continuously
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Continue antibiotics for 48–72 hours then review cultures and CRP trend  
  - If clinical improvement + negative cultures at 48h, consider stopping antibiotics  
  - If positive cultures, tailor antibiotics and treat for 7–14 days (longer for meningitis, GBS)  
  - Supportive: maintain euglycaemia, fluids (IV 0.9% saline with 5% or 10% dextrose, rate 60–100 mL/kg/day according to age and hydration)  
  - Monitor weight, urine output, electrolytes daily  
  - Reassess for complications (repeat CRP, lactate, consider LP if not done)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - If meningitis: arrange audiology follow-up, developmental assessment, consider repeat LP at end of treatment  
  - If GBS: no specific long-term prophylaxis for infant (mother may be offered prophylaxis in future pregnancies)  
  - Vaccination schedule as usual (unless specific contraindication)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline + 5% dextrose (or 10% dextrose for <3 days). Rate: 60 mL/kg/day (day 1), increase to 90–100 mL/kg/day over days 2–3 if stable. **Check local policy.**  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not routinely indicated in neonates.  
  - **Diet/NBM status:** Nil by mouth initially (until stable, then breast milk / formula when tolerated).  
  - **Observation frequency:** Hourly vital signs for first 6 hours, then every 2–4h if stable. Continuous SpO₂ monitoring if required.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (NICE-recommended):**  
  - **Benzylpenicillin (IV):** 25 mg/kg every 8 hours for first week of life; if >7 days, 50 mg/kg every 8 hours. Preterm <32 weeks: check local policy (dose adjustment for renal immaturity).  
  - **Gentamicin (IV):** 5 mg/kg once daily (term, >32 weeks). Preterm: [check local policy] (commonly 4 mg/kg every 36–48h).  
  - **Acyclovir (IV)** if HSV suspected: 20 mg/kg every 8 hours (term); preterm: [check local policy].  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - **Cefotaxime (IV):** 50 mg/kg every 8 hours (first week), then every 6 hours (avoid in neonatal units with high ESBL prevalence; consider as second-line for meningitis or severe infection).  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Aminoglycosides + loop diuretics → ototoxicity risk  
  - Ceftriaxone (AVOID in neonates) → calcium precipitation with IV calcium, kernicterus risk  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Gentamicin levels must be checked before next dose (trough <1 mg/L). Dose interval modified per level.  
  - Benzylpenicillin: adjust dose in severe renal failure (but rarely needed in neonates without anuria).  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Delaying the first dose.** Even a 30-minute delay increases mortality. Also, **check gentamicin levels before second dose** to avoid toxicity.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Meningitis (30–50% of neonates with sepsis have CNS involvement)  
  - Septic shock / multi-organ failure (renal, hepatic, DIC)  
  - Necrotising enterocolitis (NEC) (especially in preterm)  
  - Hypoglycaemia, acidosis, seizures  
  - Death (5–20%, highest in preterm and gram-negative sepsis)
- **Chronic:**  
  - Neurodevelopmental impairment (especially after meningitis)  
  - Hearing loss (post-meningitis, aminoglycoside-related)  
  - Cerebral palsy, epilepsy, learning difficulties  
  - Chronic lung disease if prolonged ventilation
sections.Prognosis:
- Mortality 5–20% (higher in very low birth weight, gram-negative or fungal sepsis).  
- Full recovery if early treatment and no meningitis/neurological involvement.  
- Long-term neurodisability in 15–30% of meningitis survivors.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always obtain blood culture before antibiotics** — but **do not delay >30 min** waiting; give after culture if at all possible.  
- **Check glucose and blood gas immediately** — hypoglycaemia and metabolic acidosis differentiate IEM from sepsis.  
- **In any unstable neonate with seizures or bulging fontanelle, do a lumbar puncture** (unless contraindicated) — you cannot treat meningitis empirically forever.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Giving broad-spectrum antibiotics without a blood culture** — you may never know the cause and risk stopping too early or missing resistance.  
- **Waiting for full septic screen before starting antibiotics** — start after blood culture; LP can be delayed if unstable.  
- **Using cefotaxime alone** (without penicillin) — it misses *Listeria* and enterococci; always combine with ampicillin/benzylpenicillin.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Refuses feeds for >2 feeds or has poor suck  
  - Temperature >38°C or <36°C (axilla)  
  - Lethargy, irritable, or unusual cry  
  - Jaundice extending to palms/soles  
  - Any rash (especially petechiae or pustules)  
  - Vomiting bile-stained contents
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Review by GP or community paediatrician within 24–48 hours  
  - If meningitis: audiology appointment within 4 weeks; neurodevelopmental clinic at 6–12 months
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU team if:**  
  - NEWS2 score ≥5 (or escalating tachycardia/hypotension despite fluid)  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (persistent after fluid bolus)  
  - GCS <15 (or not responding to voice/pain)  
  - Seizures or bulging fontanelle  
  - Worsening respiratory distress (FiO₂ requirement >0.4)  
  - Any sign of DIC (petechiae, bleeding, abnormal coagulation)
- **Call crash team / 2222:**  
  - Apnoea or bradycardia requiring cardiac compression  
  - Persistent hypotension despite 2 fluid boluses  
  - Unresponsive to pain

rawMarkdown:
## Neonatal Sepsis
**One-liner:** Bacterial infection in first 28 days of life; rapid deterioration possible; early antibiotics reduce mortality and morbidity.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Congenital heart disease (duct-dependent lesion):**  
  – **Single finding:** Cyanosis that does NOT improve with 100% oxygen (hyperoxia test fails) + differential cyanosis (lower body more blue than upper → coarctation/ interrupted arch).  
  – Immediate action: IV prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) to maintain ductal patency; review echocardiography urgently.
- **Inborn error of metabolism (IEM):**  
  – **Single finding:** Severe metabolic acidosis (base excess < –10) with hypoglycaemia, ketonuria, and normal CRP / WCC (or mild elevation).  
  – Immediate action: stop feeds, give IV dextrose (10% + maintenance rate), check ammonia, lactate, blood gas, and call metabolic team.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Temperature instability (hypothermia or hyperthermia)  
  - Poor feeding / vomiting / feed intolerance  
  - Lethargy / irritability / hypotonia  
  - Respiratory distress (tachypnoea, grunting, desaturations)  
  - Jaundice (especially within first 24 hours or conjugated)  
  - Hypoperfusion (prolonged capillary refill, hypotension)
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Pallor, mottling, cold peripheries  
  - Tachycardia or bradycardia  
  - Bulging fontanelle (late sign of meningitis)  
  - Rash (e.g., pustules → HSV?; petechiae → meningococcus / DIC)  
  - Abdominal distension / hepaticomegaly

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**  
  - **Bacterial sepsis** (GBS, *E. coli*, *Listeria*) → CRP ↑, blood culture +  
  - **Viral infection** (HSV, enterovirus, CMV) → vesicles (HSV), transaminitis (enterovirus), normal CRP initially  
  - **Fungal infection** (Candida in preterm, indwelling lines) → persistent fever, negative bacterial cultures  
  - **Non-infective causes:** IEM, CHD, NEC (abdominal distension + pneumatosis on XR)
- **Splitter:** Presence of clustered vesicles on skin or mucous membranes → think **HSV** (not bacterial); if no vesicles, bacterial is more likely.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Blood culture (ideally before antibiotics, but DO NOT delay antibiotics >30 min)  
  - Blood gas (pH, lactate, base excess, glucose)  
  - CRP (rapid bedside if available)  
  - Urine dipstick (if difficult, consider catheter sample)
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, CRP, blood culture, glucose, electrolytes, renal function, liver function, lactate  
  - Coagulation screen (if petechiae / DIC suspected)  
  - Blood gas for acid–base status  
  - Consider ammonia, acylcarnitine profile if IEM suspected
- **Imaging:**  
  - CXR (look for pneumonia, cardiomegaly, evidence of NEC)  
  - Abdominal XR (if NEC suspected — pneumatosis, portal gas)  
  - Cranial ultrasound (if seizures, bulging fontanelle)
- **Special tests:**  
  - Lumbar puncture (LP) – **strongly consider** if: suspect meningitis, neurological signs, abnormal CSF parameters; **do not delay antibiotics for LP**  
  - Urine culture (clean catch or catheter)  
  - Swabs (skin lesions for HSV PCR, stool culture for enterovirus)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** NICE guideline (NG195) — clinical risk factors + abnormal vital signs. No single score replaces clinical judgment; use the “neonatal early warning score” (NEWS2 modified) but thresholds are gestational-age dependent.
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood culture** from a sterile site (peripheral vein or peripheral line) — if omitted, you cannot confirm aetiology or tailor antibiotics.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - ABCDE approach; secure airway if depressed GCS, 100% O₂ if SpO₂ <88%  
  - IV access (difficult → intraosseous if necessary)  
  - Blood culture (then start antibiotics within 1 hour of recognition)  
  - IV benzylpenicillin + gentamicin (see Drugs & Doses)  
  - Fluid bolus 10–20 mL/kg 0.9% saline [check local policy] if shock (prolonged CRT, tachycardia, hypotension)  
  - Correct hypoglycaemia (IV 10% dextrose 2–3 mL/kg)  
  - Monitor temperature, heart rate, respiratory rate, blood pressure, SpO₂ continuously
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Continue antibiotics for 48–72 hours then review cultures and CRP trend  
  - If clinical improvement + negative cultures at 48h, consider stopping antibiotics  
  - If positive cultures, tailor antibiotics and treat for 7–14 days (longer for meningitis, GBS)  
  - Supportive: maintain euglycaemia, fluids (IV 0.9% saline with 5% or 10% dextrose, rate 60–100 mL/kg/day according to age and hydration)  
  - Monitor weight, urine output, electrolytes daily  
  - Reassess for complications (repeat CRP, lactate, consider LP if not done)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - If meningitis: arrange audiology follow-up, developmental assessment, consider repeat LP at end of treatment  
  - If GBS: no specific long-term prophylaxis for infant (mother may be offered prophylaxis in future pregnancies)  
  - Vaccination schedule as usual (unless specific contraindication)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline + 5% dextrose (or 10% dextrose for <3 days). Rate: 60 mL/kg/day (day 1), increase to 90–100 mL/kg/day over days 2–3 if stable. **Check local policy.**  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not routinely indicated in neonates.  
  - **Diet/NBM status:** Nil by mouth initially (until stable, then breast milk / formula when tolerated).  
  - **Observation frequency:** Hourly vital signs for first 6 hours, then every 2–4h if stable. Continuous SpO₂ monitoring if required.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (NICE-recommended):**  
  - **Benzylpenicillin (IV):** 25 mg/kg every 8 hours for first week of life; if >7 days, 50 mg/kg every 8 hours. Preterm <32 weeks: check local policy (dose adjustment for renal immaturity).  
  - **Gentamicin (IV):** 5 mg/kg once daily (term, >32 weeks). Preterm: [check local policy] (commonly 4 mg/kg every 36–48h).  
  - **Acyclovir (IV)** if HSV suspected: 20 mg/kg every 8 hours (term); preterm: [check local policy].  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - **Cefotaxime (IV):** 50 mg/kg every 8 hours (first week), then every 6 hours (avoid in neonatal units with high ESBL prevalence; consider as second-line for meningitis or severe infection).  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Aminoglycosides + loop diuretics → ototoxicity risk  
  - Ceftriaxone (AVOID in neonates) → calcium precipitation with IV calcium, kernicterus risk  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Gentamicin levels must be checked before next dose (trough <1 mg/L). Dose interval modified per level.  
  - Benzylpenicillin: adjust dose in severe renal failure (but rarely needed in neonates without anuria).  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Delaying the first dose.** Even a 30-minute delay increases mortality. Also, **check gentamicin levels before second dose** to avoid toxicity.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Meningitis (30–50% of neonates with sepsis have CNS involvement)  
  - Septic shock / multi-organ failure (renal, hepatic, DIC)  
  - Necrotising enterocolitis (NEC) (especially in preterm)  
  - Hypoglycaemia, acidosis, seizures  
  - Death (5–20%, highest in preterm and gram-negative sepsis)
- **Chronic:**  
  - Neurodevelopmental impairment (especially after meningitis)  
  - Hearing loss (post-meningitis, aminoglycoside-related)  
  - Cerebral palsy, epilepsy, learning difficulties  
  - Chronic lung disease if prolonged ventilation

### Prognosis
- Mortality 5–20% (higher in very low birth weight, gram-negative or fungal sepsis).  
- Full recovery if early treatment and no meningitis/neurological involvement.  
- Long-term neurodisability in 15–30% of meningitis survivors.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always obtain blood culture before antibiotics** — but **do not delay >30 min** waiting; give after culture if at all possible.  
- **Check glucose and blood gas immediately** — hypoglycaemia and metabolic acidosis differentiate IEM from sepsis.  
- **In any unstable neonate with seizures or bulging fontanelle, do a lumbar puncture** (unless contraindicated) — you cannot treat meningitis empirically forever.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Giving broad-spectrum antibiotics without a blood culture** — you may never know the cause and risk stopping too early or missing resistance.  
- **Waiting for full septic screen before starting antibiotics** — start after blood culture; LP can be delayed if unstable.  
- **Using cefotaxime alone** (without penicillin) — it misses *Listeria* and enterococci; always combine with ampicillin/benzylpenicillin.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Refuses feeds for >2 feeds or has poor suck  
  - Temperature >38°C or <36°C (axilla)  
  - Lethargy, irritable, or unusual cry  
  - Jaundice extending to palms/soles  
  - Any rash (especially petechiae or pustules)  
  - Vomiting bile-stained contents
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Review by GP or community paediatrician within 24–48 hours  
  - If meningitis: audiology appointment within 4 weeks; neurodevelopmental clinic at 6–12 months

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU team if:**  
  - NEWS2 score ≥5 (or escalating tachycardia/hypotension despite fluid)  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (persistent after fluid bolus)  
  - GCS <15 (or not responding to voice/pain)  
  - Seizures or bulging fontanelle  
  - Worsening respiratory distress (FiO₂ requirement >0.4)  
  - Any sign of DIC (petechiae, bleeding, abnormal coagulation)
- **Call crash team / 2222:**  
  - Apnoea or bradycardia requiring cardiac compression  
  - Persistent hypotension despite 2 fluid boluses  
  - Unresponsive to pain
